questions.question: What is the purpose of strategy?

responses.text: To find core of the problem and open the opportunity. Building a base for creative.
Someone who tees up creative.

To look at research, data and cultural insights to find new ways to position and answer the ask from the client — in a way that is synthesis to the rest of the team as a brief with insight to paint a creative world to play with in.
Turning the client's business problem into a simple and clear creative brief
Provide the truth / insight that's the backbone of the creative work
Planning, testing & execution of ideas
often responsible for setting the top line goal (usually in my experience commercial outcomes) and briefing channels, touch points, budget etc
Prepare the ground for fertilization
To make choices on how to compete
Provide a direction to explore for the creative team. Provide clear insights that will help solve problems. Work with the creative team to ensure their ideas lead in tandem to the insight shared.
Tell us what to say so we can come up with the most provocative way to say it.
Finding the cultural edge and approach that the creative can then leverage
Define who, what and why, and then how.
Use research to find truths so that a clear strategy can inform the creative
Brand Calculus
To point out where the treasure is buried.
gimme my armor.
to drive the client its voice
Thumb on cultural pulse
To define the problem that needs solving; bringing clarity to the category and the problem at hand.
Find the angle
Someone who provides direction and clarity. Someone who manages to understand what really matters in a clutter of information.
Connect the brief to human truths. Simplify the ask. Build a foundation for creative.
Inform the lens i view problems through; create a few new ones I hadn't thought of.
Find a unique insight that unleashes unexpected and effective creative.
uncover insights, steer creatives toward answers without providing the answers
Keep creatives creating in the right spaces.
decoder from pathways to human insights
Clarify and define the path forward for the work, define the canvas but don't paint it.
To lay the foundation of brand transformation.
Making the rest of us look smarter than we are.
give background information to product and then the current marketplace, trends and then non-marketing gibberish insights on potential consumers vis a vis what we’re advertising
Mine meaningful insights and get the idea generation process moving feeding the creatives by building roadway leading to successful campaigns
To create a strategic platform to lean into as a creative. To elevate the brief. To distill the ask and transform the business problem into a solution
Finding unique solutions to complex problems
To inspire a creative solution for client goals ultimately gaining interest from the target audience.
Create strategy, collaborate and manage collaboration across teams while ensuring alignment to said strategy
To help find the nuggets of insight and support to build our concepts from
Identify opportunities for brand amd business growth

To distil the client brief - be it a business problem or opportunity, product launch or any marketing need - into a clear and simple task that will stimulate, challenge and inspire Creatives to think of great creative ideas.
Bridge the commercial and creative, define the brief and help steer creative on right track
Get results
Effectiveness
A doctor for Brands/ A Spiritual Guide pointing you in the Right Direction
They find a territory suitable for the brand, and set the boundaries of where the creative work should live and why.
write a creative brief based on research
To create a space that makes innovative thinking inevitable.
Point the right direction
Find solutions of real problems
Create a clear and effective strategic brief
Good insights
Creating a blueprint for how to reach people and turn them into customers
A partner in crime
In the words of Mark Pollard to create an informed opinion on how to win, but alongside creatives.
Clear choices that guide execution and inspire executioners
To get to the heart of a client brief while adding a sprinkle of their own magic to inform/inspire creative.
plant the flag in the ground so the creative teams know where to dig for gold
Helping to create to most effective and clear definition of the problem to be solved and help build a framework for guiding the most effective answers
Give me the route to the goal
Map making, directional and inspiring. Where can we go?
To provide insights
To moderate all stakeholders through to agreement on one single-minded approach to solving a business problem through communications.
Sell ideas
It's there as a signpost... to set the direction of travel
To help creatives start thinking and problem solving from a unique insightful perspective

To me, the purpose of strategy is to bring useful insights into the project, that the creatives can build their work upon. Best case the stragegy delivers logical groundwork that connects briefing, brand and zeitgeist to narrow down the desired vibe for the creation process..
To build a undenyable base for the creative process.
creative ideas on point
Find Insights that are Interesting
Stealing this from someone but the best/shortest definition I've heard is "Strategy is a way to get from point A to point B."
To provide the inspiration to great creative
A position for the brand that gives it a unique advantage in the marketplace.
Define the problem and say something meaningful about how to solve it.
Combine cultural insights with what we know about the brand and consumer to set up a north star strategy for creatives to ideate against.
give the focus, purpose and direction for creation work. "The frame"
Investigation and gathering of insights to help develop great ideas for a brand
Creating surveys like this
Inspire creative thinking with nuanced, specific insights and audience truths.
Bring tha insights
Recode data
Provide insights that either spark or support creative ideas
To figure out something interesting to say about a product or a brand.
A springboard to creativity and effectiveness
Taking complex data and distilling it to inform marketing decisions
strategy should be the torch that points the way towards shared value for the audience and the brand/client

To sharply identify the immediate challenges and opportunities for a brand. To build the bridge between the client's business concerns, cultural relevance and the eventual creative solution. Simply put, they define the unique problem and get all heads around the table to nod.
To say the right things to the right people
understand consummers behaviour
To give creativity direction
Translate client problems into inspiration that inspires, helps guide and narrows creative thinking
to catalyze the creative solution to a problem
to define who we're talking to, and what we're trying to convince them of
Solve problems
Strategists are meant to find insights, particularly ones where data (what we know) and creative (what we feel) intersect.
Clearly define the problem and find the human truths that genuinely* resonate with the brand and culture (plz don't force this shit)

Business: establish a plan that informs business decisions. Creative: establish the foundation/brief for craeativr ideas and design decisions. Make sure business objectives inform creative initiatives
Simplify, focus, structure
To unearth what make people tick & bringing the tools to uplift them.
To establish the strategic positioning of a project, all the insights and true differentiators that help guide big ideas and creative expressions.
To understand briefs in a simple, clear way
it's like a "cap" for a boat, a general vaguely direction, but without instrument or sailor or a boat.... pfiut.
To set the guardrails for creative toward achieving a goal
Strategy should really nail down expectations for what the client needs regardless of what they're asking for

questions.question: What's one thing strategists could do to improve your work?

responses.text: Give tangible ideas and thinking to bounce off creatively.
Learn a thing or two about hierarchy. We need to do our homework, but we of all people should know not to create walls of words.

To outline areas, platforms, subjects, avenues to explore with data to support why. IE - Client is asking for this, but what if we explored using social in this way because we could reach XYZ in a more useful way....
Tell me 1 communication goal that's critical, then 3 other things that are nice to have.
Collaborate with creative throughout concepting
Communicate effectively with focus on priorities
Hmmm understanding that it’s a lot harder to do something than say something.
Help connect the dots so that others can logically buy in to what they emotionally do
Share knowledge
Be humble. Creative guys think we are proud and arrogant with our knowledge. Be humble and teachable.
Treat your strategy as if it will get the same level of scrutiny as the creative. If you aren't doing that, you are bound to breed resentment.
Finding true insights
Keep it simple and remarkable.
a clear strategic direction that is customer-focused
See beyond the work
Frame things in human terms (emotional, historical, personal, political).
challenge my thinking.
to be precise

involve us in the process more. I find strategy fascinating but sometimes when the solution is presented to me solved and I don't feel like I see it that way, its hard for me to think that way. I also wouldn't hate to have a strategist in a brainstorm in the first few rounds.
Stop trying to be creatives themselves.
Be clear
Distill, simplify and explain difficult stuff.
Simplify the brief.
Provide perspective. Base it in actual insight/business needs.

Simplify. There are so many factors in every brief. Product. Audience. Deliverables. channels. If a strategist can help simplify and pinpoint the one main point to convey, a creative’s jobs is 1000% easier.
create strategies that open up numerous opportunities
Fight for more research
know how to stimulate your creative team with consumer truths
Not try to be so smart, but rather be simple and concise, grounded in data and observation. Get out there and find something that creatives can't find on their own through their own creative process.
Make ideas more visually intuitive
Be a collaborator with the rest of the team and not assume their work is a black box.
answering the why
Simplify
Identify the strategic platform and keep it open to me as creative to communicate it
Challenge
Test out a brief with a creative director before it is shared

Create clear strategy. If I can run through the brief like a checklist and see who I’m making something for, where it’s going, what it needs to say and how it needs to be said, a strategist has laid the foundation for me and my copywriters and digital folks to run with it.
Finding a sweet spot to ladder creative up to
Clear summary of key findings. Suggest insights for creative execution.

Unlock amazing insights into the problem at hand. Uncovering human or cultural truths that get us thinking completely different is the one thing that can kick start great work. It's great when they make us feel uncomfortable for a bit and tap into that part of the brain they makes us want to solve something in an interesting way.
Define the area the brand should play in and give nudges in big reviews to make sure it’s doing the right things
Listen, analyse, observe
Accept and embrace irrationality / the process
Never Assume always Research
A clear and exciting territory, and a good amount of freedom.
keep the brief succinct
Find the hidden gem.
Be more bold and conclusive on their findings
Find the real concept for the solution
Identify and articulate the problem that creative needs to solve
Really good insights
Help create connective tissue between the client's goals and the agency's goals
Provide clarity
Working alongside 'designers', collaboration, no great ideas come in silo.
Build/frame expectations of cliënt so they work can land succesfully
Give a really clear brief (in a sentence) that although single minded, allows for decent creative flex and doesn't box you in.
bring a clear signular insight based on a opinion you have coming out of the research
cut through the clutter and help highlight what information matters - connect the dots
Keep the brief simple.
Find the unlock
Give actual insights, not copy and pasted from other briefs
Doing all the hard work to get to what seems to be a brainlessly simple idea/POV/approach to begin solving the problem from.
Map the playing ground
Give clear, concise and memorable direction on what the problem is, and where the solution might be found
give intelligence that makes a difference
Develop a connection between solid numbers/data/statistics and intuitive gut feelings
Provide strong insights, that can lead to creative ideas.
statistics
Give me my thoughts faster

Provide clarity and confidence. Being a creative is scary business. Good ideas are risky. Great strategy wraps brave ideas in an insulated armor that doesn't let highly literal and analytic people (smart clients) f*ck with it.
Do their job
Force a client to make choices, narrow the field, and focus on a single brand benefit.
Tell me something that challenges the conventional wisdom about the audience or situation
A thought provoking north star statement
be specific, able to deliver me essence not a massive analysis.
Ask themselves how this help the creatives develop brave ideas
Stop doing surveys like this

The more specific the brief the better. It’s not about giving us platform specs, it’s about immersing yourself in culture (don’t just leave that part to the creatives) and building on (not critiquing) creative ideas.
Make sure the work is always grounded in truthful, smart and surprising insights. Oh, and and keeping strategy upfronts to 1 or 2 slides max.
Share the process with us
Actually find unique insights that inspire
Not be prescriptive.
Really good insights
Talk with me as a creative about what they want to say. Not how they think it should sound or look like
be prepared to concept together on one hand, or embrace complete departure on the other
Keep asking the tough questions of how an execution serves the agreed upon insight.
Either show a new way of looking at the problem, or support a way I find
reveal novel powerful insights
Hone in on what matters. Restrict the possibilities.
Encourage creative solutions
Eliminate jargon, communicate in plain language instead
find more than one 'way in' to the audience we're focused on
Simplify
Offer a set of insights rich with creative opportunity.
Make it painfully simple to understand
Collaborate during the input, audit, interview, findings gathering.
Get closer to creative
Navigate through noise.
Lay out the most fruitful areas of focus in order to achieve meaningful differentiation.
SHow me a gap or something interesting. I don't need to know that 31% of GEn Z like TikTok. I need a "So what"?
Clear general direction objectives
Clarify. Goals of the business and challenges the client faces.

Speaking as an agency designer who often gets pulled into work post strategy and post UX, I think a big help would be to incorporate design value into strategy conversations whenever possible to help prime how design can advance the project. So many ideas get killed because design is often not the first part of the project and clients often have design literacy gaps. There's never enough time to fill them, or energy to create engagement for the unknown at later stages.

questions.question: What do you wish strategists did more of?

responses.text: Get to the problem and point quicker in slide decks.
Nourish the line between setting up an idea, and actually thinking of one.
Feedback with creative and as things evolve continue to tweak strategy to support creative.
Say which parts of creatives arw working well (rather than say which parts aren't)
Collaborate with creative throughout concepting
Stop it will all the jargon and speak plainly .
Collaboration
speak up
Stop sweating the words
Tbh, maybe try to STFU sometimes and not bore everyone with something you've read.
Tell me a story about the audience I am speaking to. I want to connect with a "real" person.
Summarize why and keep it interesting.
research
Be strategic
Micro-dosing.
more date, less feelings.
to be more precise
Hard to say! I think off the top of my head and after some recent events, work with us more. Is there a way to back our crazy idea into your strategy?
Stop pushing their own agenda. The work is the work. If you want the strategy to be standalone, go and work in a strat only consultancy.
Have an opinion. Put your neck on the line with it.
Be nicer when I don't understand how they work..
Depends on the strategist in question. But one thing I appreciate they do is the footwork of gathering data to support our strategic choices.
Talk less. Say more.

See previous answer: simplify. The role of the strategist is to look under very rock. This leads to teams of observations, insights and information. Lots of it is interesting. Lots of it is relevant. It’s tempting to share it all. But then, as a creative looking at too many points, it becomes hard to know the main point and the starting point.
understand the difference between a fact and an insight
Be invited to brainstorms
Break status quo
Talk to real people and get up from the computer.
Break rules & invent new frameworks
Work with account to gain better understanding of client needs not just audience needs.
be collaborative. the best strategists are, as are the best creatives
Creative thinking
Be collaborative and not pushy. Understanding where their role ends but also how to find the bridges without executing
Simplify
Bring a clear perspective based on research and truths
Ask if something is doable WITHIN SCOPE
Thoughtstarters
Clarity. Clear focused briefd

Be willing to have their strategy modified once we get thinking. There is a tendency in our industry to want to have something completely approved and the box ticked to say we have done our job. The best creative outputs can come when creatives, or other people in the agency, get hold of a brief and uncover another opportunity that might not be completely on brief, but still answers the client’s problem. The longer I work in the industry the more agile and open we all need to be to get to great work. That goes for creatives too. Prepare to have you mind changed is a great way to live!
Write simply and clearly - no need to use big clever words that don’t mean anything. Show 100+ slide decks. If it’s not simple and short creatives won’t get it.
Listening
Provide useful insights. Make choices. One strategy, one target audience. Make it ultra specific.
Disassociate from work to focus on Personal Life
Be up to date with references, and finding examples which are not only good for the brand, but also exciting and fun.
keep the brief succinct
Be a great storyteller. Make it dumber (Dan chu told me that).

Strategists usually get stuck on the agencies way of work. Means - working apart in the findings and then pass the torch. Why not find the paths together and also keep working together with creatives.
to help people not consumer
Question and challenge aspects of the client brief that ring untrue
Better insights
Distill information in a way that everyone can digest easily
Collaborate
Non linear thinking (not sure if it's just some of the strategist I've worked with)
Involving tactical experts
Cheer on the creative work. And also be super helpful about messaging, really nail that side so the creatives aren't trying to puzzle out what needs to be said and how.
involvement throughout the creative process
Less waffle.
Less information. Make it tight. On ONE page.
Collaborate

Primary ethnographic research.  It's not their fault they don't get to. Clients and agencies alike won't fund it, and agency margins are too low to allow strategists to be out wandering about talking to and observing folks.  But it is what happened with strategy/planning was at its best
Be flexible
strategy... they seem to want to do everything but strategy
Think more like how creatives approach ideas
Think more like a creative that has to work with their work.
Accept that there are many ways to fulfill the briefings.
dig deep into the target audience
Try to sell great work that is off-brief
Make it simple. Make it simpler still.
Their job
Collaborate with creative leaders earlier in the process.
Take big swings
audience research rooted in real human truths, which lay the foundation for engaging stories
synthesizing
Sit with creatives to get a better understanding of the creative process as well as help the creatives understand the strategic process.
Create a great strategy

Really understanding not just the brand’s existing audience, but opportunities in the new audiences the brand wants to reach. Strategists have as much of a right as anyone to share the cool things they see in the world and online. Inspiration is everywhere and always wanted. Statistics less so.

Being aggressively insightful. I've worked with a lot of strategists who get deflated when they realize creative tends to have the final say. My favorite strategists are the ones who push past that and stay in the game to defend the work.
Tell us how it works and whats the desire goal
Think beyond
Dig deeper into insights OR Make a hypothesis based off data.
think about the output of the brief
Treat creatives as collaborators, not executors of their will
be more prepared to embrace lateral thinking
The earlier creatives are involved in the strategic process, the better.
Proper research
identify new arenas where a brand can play and win
Brief. As in, be brief, and do briefings.
Cheer up, smile, be positive
be human

taking a stance on what message we should be sending. often I hear "it can be x, y, or z - whatever feels inspiring" which doesn't allow the team to rally behind the ideas I bring since it's so open-ended
Listen
Show their work (i.e., this study and these numbers and this trend tell us that our target demo is resonating with ideas about X).
Checking during insight forming
Consider behavioural sciences.
Open up the process to collaborate and talk through finding instead of present it all at the end.
learn how to make ads
Stop beign unclear
Ask better questions.
Consider how their strategies may come to life. I'm often expanding my knowledge of UX and strategy and always hope for the same from other disciplines.

questions.question: What's the most frustrating habit strategists have?

responses.text: Explaining things clearly that sometimes can feel like we are treating clients like dummies.
Stepping/ overstepping into the conceptual realm.
Refusing to see how a creative solution that is left of center could still hit all the marks plus even more....
Picking up minor copy / grammar / formatting points on first round review
Not being flexible when hearing other potential insights the creative team was entertaining
Bring creative comparisons from external brands
Calling something best practice lol, or bringing in previous experiences into the project that aren’t related
navel gazing
Constant re-writing of ideas, as this is not the job
The slides on data. I know it's interesting to you, just get to the insights from the data and research you've done.
Getting too far along on thought starters. It backs us into a corner, especially when the client becomes attached.
Processes for processes' sake
Using esoteric terms and acronyms.
creative briefs
Big words because you don't have concise ones
Thought-starters.
trying to do my job.
Whe they propose more than 3 communication paths.
A lot of them but you get over it
Writing loads and loads to 'set the scene'
Slavishly referencing the minutia of the research
Quoting stuff or saying rehearsed phrases that supposably are indisputable truths
Adding too many slides to a presentation deck in an effort to explain how we landed on a strategy.
Highly un-actionable feedback. Attempting to bullshit bullshitters.

Creative thought starters. It’s natural to share a creative idea if you have one. It’s easy to see how an example of creative output might be helpful. There’s significant overlap between strategy and creative. But at the end of the day l, creative thoughts starters are a waste of time at best and derail the creative’s process at worst.
strategists who think they're creatives and build briefs with an idea baked into it, instead of a guiding principle that helps creatives get to the ideas
Reiterating target audiences
the search for new insights in super worn categories
30-slide decks to make the strategy as creative as possible.
Too many words
Over explaining (e.g. stratsplaining) and assuming the team has no strategic experience.
reading a brief verbatim or giving stock answers. the most important part of a strategist is their own personal interpretations and opinions
Deep dive
Tendency to give you the communication solution instead of digging further behind it
Unsolicited feedback
marketing jargon
Promising work OUT OF SCOPE
Long decks that lose my attention
Data and information dumping
Giving too much information that clutters the brief.
Very long slide decks. Filter it down! Talking too theoretically - be more real world and practical.
Data dump
Overrationalizing ideas. Also, holding on to / guarding a strategy that just doesn't work creatively.
Multiple Perspectives and View-Points
Boring references that are "on territory"
writing overly elaborate briefs
The brief is usually ignored so I’d start there.
It's annoying when strategists beat around the bush with a bunch of information instead of being direct and conclusive.
when we take humans out of the equation and turn them into numbers and data
Including creative references in the brief—it clutters our thinking instead of sharpening it.
Too many numbers in the output
Providing too much detail
Isolated thinking
Frameworks, although integral to the process, sometimes if taken too literally can be uninspiring.
Now-it-allism
Thinking they are a creative (usually copywriter) and feeding back on things which aren't messaging/brief related but are in the ECD's terrain.
too much information presented
information overload
“These are not right, but here’s some ideas”
brainstorms
Writing pithy strategy lines
Strategic set-ups.  They're usually far, far too long.
Coming up with creative springboards that are already creative big ideas
strategists trying to creative direct, trying to make the ad in the room more like the one in their head
Share briefs that feel like client breifs

Creation has to fulfill what strategy promises. It's very easy to promise a lot and to say this or that strategy is the best. But the proof has to be delivered by the creative department. That's like strategy saying "A priest, a BMW and a dinosaur enter a bar."... and the creative department has to finish the joke.
Information overload. Makes it harder to find the important insights.
safe play
theoretization
Taking all the tiiiiiime. Yes, you want to get the strategy right but don't eat up all the creative time. Give me your C+ with time to work and collaborate not your A+ with no time left on the clock.
First thoughts
Taking weeks to "figure it out" and short-changing the time available for creative development.
Trying to write the concept in the creative brief
Focusing too much on the problem dictated by the client and not the possibilities
toooons of numerical details
Strategists creating creative platforms instead of creating strategic platforms that creatives can use to develop creative.
Trying to be intelligent

When strategists take on the role of account people and critique the creative ideas with the client “in mind.” Critiquing creative work is the job of the CD, even if your intention is to make the work better and more relevant to the client. Instead of critiquing, re-direct with thoughtful inspiration and references.
Too many strat slides.
Leaving the design to the end
Briefs. Because sometimes they limit us instead of support. They need to start thinking like a creative and then use that mindset to develop briefs
Delivering data as insight.
being overly academic
Giving me briefs where have of what they ask of me is to simply copy formulas or other ads
being thorough is incredibly important. but being thorough is different from over-spending time. spending too much time in the strategy phase often negatively impacts creative, for many reasons.
When the strategy is kept hidden from other functions until the briefing stage. Lots of relevant client conversation remains hidden that would be useful in the creative development process.
Seeing the brief as a rigid solution in itself, rather than a springboard
stay on surface
50 slides when 5 will do.
Oversharing options
Briefs that are too long/wordy
creative thoughtstarters - they should be treated as something to beat
Reviews
We all know — “thought-starters” that read more like campaign statements. Especially when they say, “but it can be anything” right after.
Overthinking it & strategy/industry jargon
Not including creative in the audit
Thinking that strategy is end point of everything we do together
Knowing-it-all.
Saving creatives from all the insights and research done along the way.
group working sessions
Volunteer yourself to infinite reporting with OKR
Asking too many questions, setting too many boundaries.

I thikn part of jargon in every field is intended to dismay others from analyzing the work. I'd like to be given strategy briefs with less jargon to understand the concepts and ideas faster. Design suffers from this too.

questions.question: What do strategists never think about?

responses.text: The design of slides
Creative writing.
Whats beyond data, where is the next trend, where can we really take larger swings and risks.
Client's internal policies
They themselves are consumers/users, and their opinions based on personal experiences are valid
the target audience
biscuits in the meeting
The business implication of your ideas
Ermmmm
Their role as a collaborator beyond the creative brief to help us stay the course without raining on the parade.
"I'm a creative"
The intangibles that affect feelings, like craft and mediums..
Buying me lunch
"How is this funny?"
we're in this together.
the want all in one creativity
Although its good to have boundaries creatives hate being restricted.
That maybe, just because they can write, poetic prose is not what's needed from them.
Amongst research I find the emotion can be lacking
Hard to answer. One thing I wish would be considered is the amount of time it takes to convert a strategy into creative.
Brass tacks. Thinking it and actually selling it are two different things.

Tough question, since every strategist thinks, operates, and conveys information in a unique way. One answer would be a strategist needs to be flexible and see flexibility as part of the job. It’s never one type of deck or one approach for every project. Sometimes 100 page deck is what’s needed. Sometimes one sentence on a slide. Sometimes strategy is a kick off point for creative and other times strategy is rationale for selling through creative work, so I guess my answer is understand your role will change based on what’s needed to make the best creative.
How creatives will interpret their briefs
I am strategist. I don´t know.
That they are a service to the creative team, not their own team with their own agenda.
The career objectives and needs of the client who's bringing this work to us. Just because the insights are solid doesn't mean it's serving the real purpose.
putting things in bullshit monitor. too much marketing speak.
Human emotions
Good strategy they do think about the most important things
If their work will be understood if they aren't in the room
keep the brief brief — so many are a client research dump. Get to the point!
SCOPE
How to digest information, there always seems to be an overload of information
Provide a framework for tactical creative execution
Brevity is your friend
Practicality
Disabilities
An insight that's actually stron
Life outside the Agency
To think like people, not like brands.
how to keep the brief succinct
The creative potential or limitations of thier briefs.
I would say they overthink about “respecting” the creatives and that they shouldn’t contribute to the creative work.
in my humble opinion, he does not put himself in the place of the consumer on the human side
What is the problem that the day-to-day client/approver is really trying to solve? What are their personal motivations that will influence the way they evaluate the work.
Less is more
Brevity
Being a creative
Speaking to people who don't look like them.
Creatieve strategists only think of message, not experience/value
Is this work actually creatively compelling/exciting, or does it just 'fit the brief'?
art direction!
media
The brief can always evolve
Nothing jumps to mind here.
Competitors are not only direct competitors but all kinds of alternatives.
giving a history of the brand / category... we rarely get an understanding of the direction of travel of the brand
They sometimes feel like they are briefing business people not creative minds.

To realize that good, intuitive creation can live without any strategy at all. But even the best strategy is useless, when it doesn't lead to artwork, ads, spots, texts, that you can actually use out there.
The consequences of their strategy for the creation process.
actually TALK with the target audience
What the edges of possibilities are and where to be unconventional
Tough one. Strategists think of everything. Perhaps think about being illogical more often. Like Rory Sutherland's "Alchemy" point of view. Think about what doesn't make sense sometimes.
Their job
Divorcing "brand strategy" from "media strategy" was the dumbest thing we ever did. If the strategy doesn't acknowledge where and how it's going to be seen, it's useless.
is that an insight or a campaign idea?
Will your insights provoke interesting concepts?
bring me coffee ;) kidding, but noo we have great strategists, they think of all
How will this land with the creative team, and will help them develop ideas.
How to write a great brief for the CREATIVE TEAM
Often strategists consider themselves allies with the account team, when in reality they should always be on the side of the creative.
I don't know, all the strategists I work with are pretty thoughtful.
Real people vs numbers
That they are not the target most of the time.
Production logistics of ideas they have
that creative thinking can be strategic thinking from a different starting point.
It's not a baton pass. They can stay invested throughout the development of the work.
Maybe that people really don’t care that much
They have a responsibility on the quality of the creative product
How people actually behave.
Having fun
understanding the mentality of how & when our audience is interacting with the idea. should we hit them when they're scrolling and bored? When they're exchausted on the way to work? etc
relatability

The creative is an expression of the strategy. The strategy is not the creative. Willingness to live in a nuanced space and to be experimental can be difficult for a data-driven researcher, but essential to bridging the gap to be a great strategic partner for creatives.
how you deliver the brief matters - make it fun
Time for additional validation with client
Broad human context
Time management of executive teams.
How their work translates to forming creative foundations.
How work appears in the world, not judged by people in offices with perfect lighting
be clear and neat
How their strategy helps solve the problem or accomplish the goal.
Not sure

questions.question: What is the purpose of a creative brief?

responses.text: Clarity and inspiration
Create a pinnable summary of the task at hand.

To outline the problem we are solving with data and cultural insights that paint the way of where to explore - while outlining the table steaks, and importantly the brief needs to outline scope and fidelity needed.
To ensure the communication goal is as clear and simple as possible.
Backbone of creative concepting
Condense an idea, project objective into simple, workable format
the foundation of setting a creative up to do their job. It’s not a visual reference guide
to be the true north
Make creatives think about the problem they are solving
Tbh, just a way to ensure the creative guys don't call me every hour for clarity, since they have a clear brief now.
Capture these essence of what we need to say and to whom to achieve our client's objective
Defining and summarising the creative need, approach and insight
To make the job clear, and inspire me.
I gave up on those a long time ago. I'd rather just ask the questions I need answered.
Inspire and provide meaningful constraints
To direct the treasure hunt. It's a map.
boosting the brain.
to drive the creative team
To inspire big ideas within the realm of budget, audience, and the core of the brand/campaign.
To inspire and inform, and be the foundation of the work that follows.
Define the angle, get everyone’s buy in. The creative brief is the first piece of creative work.
Providing a clear direction for the creative work. A perfectly thrown ball. All we need to do is catch the thing and run.
Inspire creative and give it reason.
Inform/inspire/act as a contract.
To unlock original, unexpected, effective creative work.
to provide the context that shapes the ideas
To inspire creative work that’s on brief
Inspiring and focus the creatives
Create a derisked creative environment where the client and agency are aligned about where to focus.
A shorthand for the problems that need solving
To give creative the confidence to do their work and not pepper account with a million questions.
to give insights on how consumers might be thinking or how this product can relate to them
To deliver a compelling thought provoking campaign that maximizes the value delivery to customers
To solve a business problem
To set a foundation for innovative and relevant ideas
to inspire a path to action
To establish a foundation for creatives and now digital strategists and social media managers to build content from
To have a guide and Bible to check work against and explain all client needs, info and more
Aling the creative task. Set practical boundaries for do’s amd dont’s
To crystalise the problem in a way that provokes thought and leads to creative ideas.
Define the territory the creative should play in - act as a springboard for ideas.
Insight
To set a direction that guarantees both effect and creativity.
Visual Manifestation of addressing Brand Challenges
It's the field and rules of the game you're going to play.
provide insights that express themselves in unique creative
To unlock possibility.
Highlight the client’s problem pointing the direction (or directions) for the solution.
To find the problematic
Identifying the problem that needs to be solved by the creative team and illuminating the pathway to solving it.
A good nerve or truth that's tackled and brought to the audience in in an unspoiled and clear way.
To create a clear path toward success for a brand, that everyone then follows
Be clear
Creating guard rails in-line with the strategy, but not bondage. Allow creatives to be imaginative and tell the strategy story through visuals.
A broad direction that drives associative thinking in a rich direction
To know we're in right space for the brand/org, while still allowing creative to push into exciting spaces.

create clarity not just for the internal teams, but to create a single source of truth for onboarding freelancers/photographers/videographers/editors/etc to all work from when brought into the process.
Ground us all in the ask and light a fire of inspiration
The chance to create with substance rather than just ‘taste’
Something for us to refer back to when we are lost
To give us a North Star to work towards
Align stakeholders around the key job-to-be-done and the key way-in, via comms.
Map the playing ground
it tells me what the problem is, and suggests a place to start digging for an answer
To open doors for the mind.
To provide clear details about the brand, the product, the audience and the desired outcome. How to get there should be part of the strategy and the creativeprocess.
Set the playing field for strategy & creation
guideline
Cut the crap
Align on a provocative way to get from Point A to Point B. The brief will tell us what we need to tell the audience. The creative will tell us how we should say it to the audience.
A jumping off point
What do you want your target audience to DO as a result of exposure to the campaign?
find a way to challenge the conventional wisdom about audience, brand, situation, et cetera
To detail the strategy and set up the creative team for success. Also to get alignment.
give a platform
To deliver only the information that is necessary while also giving a tight strategic platform that the creatives can build on.
Give context and parameters

To build a solid jumping off point with all of the necessary information needed for the work. Both the boring stuff served to us by the client, but also the interesting stuff that a client would never provide an agency. It’s a foundation and a reference point that we should be able to test our ideas against to know if they’re working or not. Often when a brief is too wide/vague that is fundamentally impossible to do, as every idea could technically be “on brief.”

To take the goals of the client brief and find the cultural way in that unlocks something interesting for the creative team to run with. When I get a brief, I'm looking for things like truly unexpected consumer problems, cultural truths, relevant tribe behaviors that get us thinking beyond demographics, etc.
To understand the project deeply
To inspire creative ideas with insights that make work client-proof
Unfortunately, to have one document that everybody can agree on. Instead, it should be a creative contract.
excite and liberate great, effective work
To assist/guide me to the desired end result of a piece of creative
it's a jumping off point for collaboration...not a millstone
To form the bridge between a client's business needs and the creative solution.
Point you in the right direction
spark media neutral relevant creative solutions
To distil the problem creative has to solve. And who we have to solve it for.
Inspire the best creative solution
To inspire great creative through an insight
To define what we're trying to achieve, with supporting mandatories and guardrails to keep in mind before we start thinking of ideas (not after we present them)
Define the problem clearly and provide a solution
A brief sets the tone for the project, uncovers unique and actionable insights rich with creative opportunities, and clearly lays out the business goals, technical requirements, and deliverables.
Feel inspired and informed
Alignment
To identify problem
To truly understand objectives & have a solid starting points. No doubts & questions.
Raw materials (not a kit of parts, nor an instruction manual). There’s still a shit ton of work to make a table from a stack of finely cut boards.
give me a simple, clear instruction
Be clear and concise on the direction
To state the frustrations of the client and the goals the company hopes to achieve.
To convince the creative team that strategy has done everything they can to convince the client of the best possible project direction first, to share action items for how to get it done second.

questions.question: What info is often left out of briefs?

responses.text: what behavior shift or change do we want to see and how can we credibly do it
True insights
Not much, usually. Bit sometimes strategists don't dig into the history of the company/brand, or the details of the product enough.
The business-problem, the goals, the single-minded proposition
the history, the evidence, the audience, the direction
How other brands approached the same shallenge.
A specific direction or real insight. Saying let's make a "human-centric, holistic campaign that guides the audience" isn't a strategy. It's just bullshitting.
That the strategist had 3 weeks, while the creatives are supposed to be ready in 1 week :D
what should be the end result
There‘s too much of it actually
A real point of view. The facts are there but the leap from data to daring decision is where the magic happens.
Prop, unique insight
Are the channels wide open or do we already know where this needs to run? When is it due? What is the budget?
the real business goal or problem we're solving
cultural insights
cool fun behavioral facts about TG
What the client doesn't need or want.
Real insights
Human truths. Brand truths that you can’t just google. Cultural insights that a relevant to today. Don’t just hit us with “fearless girl” as an example.
I love a brief that's bold enough to declare a "single most important thing" — rare to see a brief that includes that.
Clients expectations
Real human truths sometimes I see the same stats and info brief after brief that seems more random survey than human truth
The human behaviors of the target audience. Idiosyncratic stuff that makes them human.
an exciting insight
Why they made decisions they did
creativity is often found at the source...so while the brief should be short, the background info should be made available too. up to me how deep i want to go.

Little side comments or biases that the client brought up in the process of developing the strategy. These often feel unrelated to the brief, but can provide helpful watch outs and context when developing creative.
Budget, timings, previous rounds of dev
Actionable facts and insights
It's more than too much information is left in. The problem is distillation, or a lack thereof.
Audiences are so nuanced today but still neglected
Powerful insights
What the client is expecting - even if it's wrong, it dictates how we present and pitch ideas
client expectations
The actual insight. How did we arrive there? What are the data sets? How does it apply to the creative expectations? Etc.
Broad context of human behavior
Consideration of decision sciences (of clients) and the actual implementation.

Creative brief? Where can I get one of those? Often, working internally, it’s assumed that everyone is already clued into what’s needed. That and the range of projects tends to create the common (and sometimes valid) question, “does this really need a brief?”
budget, timeline, audience nuances
they forgot themself how should be clear what they say
Why this is the proposed strategy. How the strategy will achieve its desired results with the help of expert creative tactics.

What is the client doing wrong now? This might feel redundant or even confusing, but context like that can be helpful when designing, giving the idea we're pushing and also the mistakes being made that are driving those thoughts.

questions.question: What's the best way to give feedback on creative work?

responses.text: Verbal with a bullet pointed list followed up.
Like a relationship. Always remember that you are on the same team.

Feedback should be collaborative. So when strat give feedback it should be constructive and should also offer to help find XYZ or provide data or something that moves things forward, rather than just making the team go back to the board. The feedback should be supportive.
What's working well, what could work if tweaked, and what should be left. Always with rationale.
Collaborative feedback
Hear what the creative has to say about what he did, & why, then scrutinize that based on the creative brief/laid down objective
With rationale and coming at it from the eye of the consumer
truthfully
Meaning and aesthetics
Understand that I spent hours to come to that point. Try to be reasonable in your feedback
Take on the role of the persona and ask inquisitive questions that help us defend our choices. If we can't then it becomes obvious we aren't there yet.
Constructive, contextually relevant, and candid.
Separate the strategy from your personal design opinion.
Play "yes, and"
real talk. keep it short but relevant.
what creative team are going to think about the brief
Why it doesn't work and how it could if *blank* was changed. That goes more for CD's than strategists.
In relation to the brief. Not personal opinions.
Find the good in everything. New ideas are fragile so they must be treated with care if they’re to flourish.
Rip the band aid. Ask the hard questions early.
Concisely with as few buzzwords/little bullshit as possible.

Is the creative good? If the creative doesn’t ladder back to strategy, can strategy to retrofit to help sell through the creative? When pitched to clients, how can we use strategy to get the clients to buy this idea before they’ve even seen it?
depends on the creative team, but it is to ask subtly to see the backing of the idea.
How can we make this more meaningful for the audience? How this could work... Positive constructive talk.
Rooting feedback in strategic imperatives rather than subjectivity
To be collaborative and open with creative, recognize their efforts but also be firm about what needs to be done.
honestly. let’s tear down silos at this point.
One to one feedback
Is this on brief? Is this on strategy? Of not how many points are we hitting and what’s missing? Could the missing pints generate a second routes
In a post review document released 12 hours after the creative has been shown
Start with the positives, be direct and don't suggest a solution.
I love notes on a PDF/marking it up. Oftentimes strategists try to use creative terminology but use it incorrectly and changes that weren’t needed get made and Vice versa
In person feedback is always the most productive
How can we… add, create, include, improve… based on the audience?

Probing the thought process that led to the idea (especially if they don’t like it or agree with it) rather than outright dismissing a direction or thought. This in turn can unlock other ways to approach or tweak the idea to make it stronger, or really expose that the direction is not correct. Both lead to better results.
Directly and to the point
Document or recording
Ideas should be judged based on the strategy. But if the strategy isn't clear, it becomes difficult to judge the ideas. I wouldn't worry about the way to give feedback. Worry about a decent strategy.
Always being able to visualise ideas
We don't care about what you don't like. We care about what works and doesn't, and WHY.
in-person to talk over then on email to refer back to during edits/updates
How does this make the brand more relevant to our audience?
Analyzing wether the ideas are heading in the right direction or guiding them into.
quality studies and user experience
Candidly, clearly, and compassionately. Respect the effort but don't hem and haw around killing ideas.
Just radical honesty measured against the insight or any well-thought "metrics"
Verbalized in the clearest terms possible and followed up by written record for reference.
Voice of the target
Straight up, no time wasting, but constructive, 'have you thought about this, what if we try this'. A straight up No doesn't cut it.
Don't direct, applaud & challenge selectively

Talk it out first - but remembering you're not the ECD. Although you're allowed to have an opinion on creative as is everyone, the problem is when it strays in as though it's not an opinion but feedback that must be actioned. When actually, it's subjective and creative feedback rather than strategic.
happy for live feedback but a follow up after with bulletpoints is ideal
Where in the idea is effective/ineffective in answering the brief
Not by email
What could get sharpened
Face yo face
In-person. Face to face. Conversationally.  Followed up by simple notes.
This doesn't solve the business-problem for specific reasons. This idea could be tweaked in this specific way to solve the business-problem.
yes and...
Say why it's off breif and suggest how you can save an idea if it's good.

Communicate on eyelevel. Not from above. Yes, strategy comes first. But that doesn't mean it might be adjusted along the project. The relationship between strategy and creatives should be an open pingpong. Both can be right but still follow different paths. It's important to find the best way together.
honest, direct, personally
not judgmental
helping to create it
Anything that starts with, "This is good and could be even better if we add _____". Build on the idea. Make it better.
What the stats say about the approach
In writing. Dumping your half-baked thoughts in a meeting means creatives have to unscramble it. Be clear and methodical with any feedback.
Kindly and candidly
honest conversation. Make sure to ask the why and listen before dismissing an idea.
is it strategist's role to provide feedback on creative?
Specific, thought out feedback that's based on the strategy, what they know about the client and what feeling they get from the work
Comments that make the work stronger

Instead of shutting down an idea, re-direct it. Would the concept be better on tiktok? Is there an artist that did something similar in a cool way? Do you have a learning from another project that would be relevant? Base feedback in facts, not opinions. Especially not the assumed opinions of your account team or client.

For discussion things, face to face > zoom > call... For tiny things, email > slack > doc comments. If I have to send a written reply of any length more than once about creative feedback, I'm walking to your desk or we're jumping on a call.
Written
Pushing creative work is good, challenge with helpful soultions
"This idea is on strategy because:____" or "This idea is off strategy because:____"
in writing
Feedback that assists in guiding a team to the proper decisions
constructively, with a focus on what feels most exciting --- and the challenges/builds that will make it better
Small, casual chats, rather than multi-department group check ins.
Objective
what if
Quick chat. Honest but respectful. Clear suggestion for improvement.
Talking
consolidated, written pre read with time to discuss after
As a question in discussion - have you considered X? safe to assume we have a reason for choosing words, channels, ideas, etc.
objectivity
Clear, direct, concise, backed by solutions.
Be part of creative checkin
Be part of strategy sessions
In-person communication, bi-weekly meetings.
Contextually. Get into the mix and let’s generate discussion. Make time to talk and share ideas.
Head- -hearts - body format
A website showing the right direction
Your feedback/input belongs on the brief. I only accept feedback from the CD.
Referencing strategy docks

questions.question: What's the least helpful feedback you've received?

responses.text: The visuals or designs aren’t right early on in the process
Don't highlight problems unless you have ideas for solutions. Words for everyone in life. But mostly strategists when responding to creative.
That its wrong or misses the mark. Strat, should show us where or why it is not meeting the standards outlined in the brief.
"Maybe".
“Try to think out of the box”….like what is thatt????
Things they personally don’t like lol
I dont like it
Is it on strategy?
About design. Lmao
"This is too bold." - your job isn't to mitigate risk. Quite the opposite. You should be working with us to help bold feel safe to the client.
Subjective, reductive, inconsiderate – anything that doesn't push the work forward.
I find that sometimes I get a lot of deep information, which isn't bad, but it's often not the right information I need to figure it out.
The media plan.
how he would write the headline.

I think its all useful in one way or the other. The more insight the better. You may not use it initially but its always good to look back at a little nugget of strategy and justify your work with it.
That they don't like it / or that they were 'expecting something braver' with not further context. Especially when the strategy is generic.
“It doesn’t work”
"I love it"
Personal opinions. Often we're not the consumers of the products we sell. Keep your work hat on, save the rest for the bar.
If they like it or not.
Whether they like it or not
that this creativity is beautiful
I saw someone else do this and it didn't work.
I like it
Refer to the briefing.
‘here’s the bad ad’ don’t do that.
Maza nahi aa raha hai
If they feel it or not. Personal opinion at this stage can not be taken
That it will only live in formats they've decided it will
How to do your job.
Art direction. Please don’t take my design and try to re-lay it out. We creatives do think about things and use reasoning for our decision making.
“I’ll know it when I see it.”
This is the truth
That it's not on brief.
Theoretical complex stuff
Specific design details
A mixed answer or doubts. Make it a yes or a no.
"This isnt it, I dont feel it"
"I don't think I like this" "It's too much"
their opinion
The number of banners and emails you’ll need to make.
That the ideias should be more safe
the budget
"This will be hard to sell the client on." If it's on-brief then you should be an advocate for getting client ot buy it.
Somehow I haven't been "treated" by much of nonsense
Providing comms planning too late in the game
How to execute
Anything beyond concept execution
Again just when they're behaving like the ECD rather than strat.
a subjective thought about a tactical idea, specifically for pitches.
I don't like it/don't think the client will like it - with not context of why or how we might overcome that
“I don’t like it”
That they've seen it before
Personas are bullshit

It's "off strategy" without a bullet-proof reason why. Sometimes strategist's egos get triggered when you stray from their proposition. This is especially true for strategists who are frustrated creatives. So if they don't see their idea/words in the work, it's "off strategy".  Super frustrating. But tends to be the domain of younger, less experienced strategists.
I don't like it. It's not creative. It's not on brief.
I don't like it
Say that its off breif and not come with a solution to help save it.
Critizing without offering any useful thought, how the creative work should look like.
Act like an untouchable client instead of being a cooperative teammember.
make it bold
It‘s perfect!
It's just not there yet. Or, "i'm not the creative but...".
Give wip props
Whether they like it or not.
"The client won't like this."
that creatives can't help inform the strategy
how it should be done ;)
I don't get it
Any ‘starter’ ideas
Audience Americans M-F (18-49). That they don’t think the creative will “go viral.”

Not sure there's one "least" thing, but I think it's really unhelpful when strategists are inflexible about how the work evolves. I think the best work comes when the outputs from strat and creative are fluid.
“Make it sexier”
This doesn’t fit the brief lol
Their opinion. (Whether they "like" it or not)
lots of unncessary info
Make it engaging or make sure to look at the references!
i'd rather they ask questions to challenge it, rather than tell me about it
When they get subjective and speak like they are a creative. It's important for them to stay in their function and give their thoughts on the strategic thinking and validity of an idea.
I don’t like it, but not sure why
The client will not buy this!
That it doesn't conform to the brand archetypes they spent bloody forever pointlessly working on
The brief might not happen
non specific feedback that doesn’t connect back to the brief
if they personally don't like an idea - and are not/don't understand the target audience. example: I'm a woman and have had male strategists give feedback that an idea won't resonate with women.
appreciation
“I don’t like it.” Or, “we’ve done that before.”
Its not on brief (lol)
How to create.
It feels like we deviated from the brief a bit here.
vague audience insights. I need something clear and human, not "44% of users use apps daily"
They never feedback on is the strategy been right, because the strategy because the strategy always changes depending on the mood.
Don’t tell me about the creative. Clarify the problem or the goal.
Not sure

questions.question: What should strategists keep in mind about creative teams?

responses.text: They might not get it right first time.
We care about making things
Do be afraid of off brief ideas
That the strategy often evolves as the creative evolves. Not always. But be open to it happening.
The creatives are the creatives, not the strategists.
that they start with a blank sheet of paper, every day.
Creatives are like kids in a candy store.  Lure them with creative strategies.

Dumping tons of headlines, personas and surveys on the table isn't helpful, when you didn't find any insights in them. That's just pretending to be productive, while leaving it to the creative team to connect the dots.
Sometimes the idea comes first – and the strategy has to be reconstructed. It's not the best way, but sometimes it's like that.
you can always go smaller but never bigger
That we are all individuals with preferences and processes
The best strategists are creatives at heart. The best creatives are strategists at heart. We are all the same. Let's stick together.
They fuel the team and are not the team
Your job is to come up with the "what," and the creative's job is to come up with the, "how." You don't have to love the "how" they come up with.
we fucking love good briefs
We don't want the solution
we cannot work until they are done, so don't change strategy a day before the pitch
The creative process is messy and often doesn't have an agenda.
Step aside when it is time for the creative work

Every creative team is fundamentally different. If it’s your first time working with them, it’s best to observe the team dynamic before going hard fast. Me and my partner love working hand in hand with our strategists, including the ideation phase. Others don’t feel the same.
We're all on the same team.
We need briefing
Anything is possible, sometimes the best ideas come from making work that scares you
That they think of themselves as armchair strategists.
being clear in what is expected
Don’t promise things of us to the client. Talk with us
recognise that creatives each have their own ways of working, so adapting to what they need to be at their best is important.
We take our work personally and when we get defensive, it's because we care about what we do alot.
Engagement and entertainment are really important
we are one team
They don't find the answer. They aim the gun but don't press the trigger.
Bring the energy
we love a strategic foundation upon which to build our work

it's really vulnerable to come up with and share ideas to a group of people, especially when they depend on taste or subjection. whenever creatives are presenting work, they're sharing a personal idea that started deep inside them, which is one of the most difficult things to do EVERY day.
Be the guide!

Creatives are sensitive, stubborn, fragile, egotistical, caring beings. We want to make great work and constantly search for amazing tools to help us. Provide those tools! Be a resource, not a bottleneck! Trust us the way you want us to trust you!
Is this inspiring?
We’re going to do our own research anyway
Creatives really write strategy.
Some creatives are strategic as well.

The best creatives think strategically too. We all angle at the same objective, even if it comes from a different orbit. Creating more space to talk through how we all connect the dots and being open for a less rationale / rigid structure tends to create the most ground-breaking work.
keep it short. always too much info
Creativity is the quicksand
You are all in it together.

If you ever in disagreement with a (good) designer, just know that we're very driven to bring your ideas to life but want to be proud of and have confidence in what we're making. Pushback from designers is often because we haven't been convinced of your ideas yet! Design is the visual manifestation of strategy and development and is always the first and sometimes only thing to be judged.

questions.question: How do you judge a brief?

responses.text: The shorter the better.
Can I do something with it
If I’m inspired
Good: You immediately start having ideas.  Bad: You don't. Bad briefs are often strategically correct but creatively useless.
Short, precise, surprising
can I immediately write something against it
Do I feel inspired after being breifed?
It should be on point. Providing just the info the team REALLY needs to know. Nothing more, nothing less.
The less confusing, the better.
by the length
If it‘s interesting and there‘s something new in it
Single minded proposition. Did you successfully keep the client from packing in way too many dots to connect.
The lenght and the insight
One page, hyper focused, clearly defined desired outcomes, has an actual strategy/position to set the brand apart.
if it tells me nothing new, it's not good
if it is provocative or not. If it helps uncover a unique proposition.
specific but not leading
Do I start thinking of ideas as I'm reading it.
If it inspires great creative work

It’s length. It’s emphasis on strong references without forgetting specs. Nuanced, surprising audience insights I wouldn’t learn with a quick google. REALISTIC examples. Again no fearless girl for an organic Instagram post brief.
The main thing I need from a brief is one rock solid, surprising, truthful insight. If I can't find that on the page, I push back really hard on my strat team to make the brief better.
Detail
If a bunch of ideas start popping up in my head during the meeting. That’s a good brief.
If it makes you feel something.
is it exciting and liberating
A good one is perscriptive yet leaves room for creatives to be creative
two things: whether it shows a depth of thinking on the part of the strategist, and whether it gives me an excited feeling (where a phrase or observation or proposition gives me energy).
If it feels specific, culturally relevant and urgent--it's good. If I've seen the identical brief many times in my career, it's not.
If it makes me think differently then that’s great, but otherwise a good starting point is fine
There is a new angle and it triggers spontaneous thoughts
If I can understand it. If it's simple. If it's on one page.
It excites me
Jargon is bad, a strong insight is excellent

a good brief brings me an angle to a problem that I wouldn't have thought about or known. and it gives me information about the mindset of our audience. a bad brief references everything that's been done in an attempt to copy it, not beat it.
clarity of purpose
Can I work immediately? If so, it’s probably good. If not, the strategist has been too prescriptive and/or too vague in their presentation of the insights and information.
No bullshit, but inspiring at the same time
Through contents.
Does it generate more ideas than it does questions. And can I understand the main points without a sliver of doubt.
Is the specific task clear? Is the insight clear? The clearest instruction is the best brief
the scarcity of adverbs and adjectives.

If I have to ask a single question you’re wasting the time of the entire creative department. We judge you by the clarity of the brief. Is the information complete? Is the desired result clear? If either is no. Stop. The brief needs more work.
Does it have a clear sense of how the project ends as far as deliverables, where it lives in the client ecosystem, what expectations are for expanding the work down the road.

questions.question: What should a strategist's role be after the briefing?

responses.text: keep it on brief.
stop being annoying
Checking in and asking if the strategy is working
Collaborate. Riff. Guide. Help figure out how to sell-in the work to all the various stakeholders.  They're almost like a coach, along with the Creative Director.
Feedback ideas, reverse-engineer a strategy to sell a great idea
to look at work objectively... to ask "will this work" rather than "is this the work I'd have made"
Keep feeding the creatives with insightful ideas and thoughts as the minds start thinking.

To pingpong with the creative team – and to tell them, when their ideas go off the track. However, in the creative process it might turn out that the first strategy doesn't match with the best idea. In this case it's the responsibility of strategy and creation to connect their worlds.
Feedback the creative team, but also be open for strategy adjustments, if it makes sense to support a different idea.
help and guidance
Make it happen, frame it, help with delivering and selling the work and track whether it was any good in business terms
Be flexible. If a new idea beats the brief, help us sell pivot in. Stay close. Don't let us go falling in love with something that doesn't solve the problem.
Champion and help drive the narrative of the work
Be available for questions. Give feedback to the CD (not the teams) if the work strays too far off brief. Help sell the work.
idea bouncer offer, and in the best cases, collaborator
to make sure the strategy has informed the creative
provide extra info if asked for
To keep them true to the strategy.
Step away

To be able to answer any questions teams have during the ideation process and be a sounding board for whether the ideas are on brief. (Not whether you personally like them or not, just whether they are on brief!)

I like working with strategy almost as like a second partner, so I push to keep strategists in all reviews, making sure they're keeping an on whether the work is on brief — or if the work drifts off brief, that we can retrofit a strong, insightful reason why.
Punctual feedback
Help support the creative
To ensure that any creative ideas presented are on strategy and on target. ONLY THIS.
keeping the creatives honest to the brief
Stay out of our way till we can launch created ads and get data for them
ideally: a trusted sense-check, a potential collaborator...not a gatekeeper.
To continue to be a lens and give big picture reminders that serve to sharpen the work.
Build a case to support the creative work
Making sure strat, account and creatives are all aligned on the ambitions
A sounding board. A co-creator to make sure the work is respecting the strategy.
Be a creative
To help refine creative work per the brief
to build a case for why the ideas are doing the job they're supposed to, validate and defend the work
guide

Be a resource! Creatives are intuitive, so be available to dive further into the research, find more examples and stack the pitch ammunition for ideas as they evolve. Support, don’t fight, the creative teams!
Support support support
Help keep team on course strategically
To stay open
To stay in the loop, reflect & redirect.
A collaborative partner. Someone to help keep us creatives honest to the objective we’ve all endeavored on together.
should be to monitor the work, but this hardly happens
Helping following a direction, not create be control freak
Assuming the brief is good, stay out of the way. "Never tell people how to do things. Tell them what to do and they will surprise you with their ingenuity." -George S. Patton
To support understanding of the strategy, to be willing to work through some of the creation pain points the way that designers go through QA with developers
Say more with less words
Just be nice. We're all living creatures.
Your job is not done when the brief is presented, your job is also not to punch holes in the creative. Work to continue to find ways to support creative.

The most annoying strategist I knew though of himself as a philosopher. He just spoke in long-winded vagueries about higher order ideas. Never gave concrete direction on anything. 

Don't be a twat like him.
Strategists can be gems to creatives if they collaborate harmoniously
read stratscraps
Again, try to shut up once in a while
Keep going 🤘
You need us more than we need you. Sorry. Not sorry.

We're on the same team. Working towards the same goal. If our idea doesn't align with your strategy its not because we weren't listening or didn't understand. It's because we have 400 people giving their opinion on everything down to the pixel size of our font. Gentle reminders are welcomed and context is always encouraged. Strategy to me, is one of the most interesting parts of the brief and unfortunately although its essentially the foundation of the work, it often gets buried. Doesn't mean its not appreciated. :)

IF YOU WANT TO BE CREATIVE DIRECTORS THEN BE JUNIOR CREATIVES FIRST AND WORK YOUR WAY UP. OH WAIT, YOU DON'T WANT TO DO PLACEMENTS AND BUILD A PORTFOLIO?AND HAVE PEOPLE CRITIQUE YOUR IDEAS DAY IN AND OUT? HUH.
You’re in a creative role. Be creative with your view point.
Thank you. I love you.

This is Chicago, we don't care if you're from NY/LA. The more you mention it; the more of an asshole you sound like. You'll be judged on substance here, not style. So bring it. Good creatives do a lot of what you do naturally; help us arrive at new ways to approach the problem we're trying to solve. You get to critique the work and your perspective is valuable. So is a creative's when it comes to the brief. So if you expect thick skin, develop a bit of your own.

There’s a reason a strong strategy department is a hidden ingredient to the best, most creative agencies in the world. When done right, your work not only leads to better ideas, it helps sell the ideas to clients. Understand the power you have and how best to use it and never take it for granted.
calm your ego

You need to realize that academics that obsess over planning/strategy (there a couple planner bros that are the leading voices in the field) are a bit disconnected from reality right now and haven't been doing the actual work in a while. They want you to obsess over being a strategist and frameworks and planner struggles etc because then they can build their community and sell courses etc. You should obsess over creativity and spend more time hanging out with creatives than you do other strategists.
Strategy need to become more creative

The clients talk to strategists of various stripes all day, every day. They don't always know what your work is or why they're paying for it when they believe they've already supplied us with business objectives, positioning, and consulting strategy work. Help explain to them what strategy means in the creative context and how it builds on the services they've already paid for from other parties.
be kind and collaborative. also don’t take shit from creatives who think they’re smarter than everyone or have their own agendas
Strategist should regularly sit with creatives and be a part of their brain storming and creative processes
Let’s work more together
You are only as good as the people that can understand you
I have found that planners are much more collaborative and brief decks inspire much more than a white piece of paper with too much info.

WE ARE THINKERS AND DOERS TOO. Just because I didn’t explain every design choice I made, doesn’t mean I didn’t think about why the legal type is that big, why the logo is not in the bottom right, why a certain set of colors was used and not another etc etc etc. We think about shit, and we know how to connect it to your strategy. It’s literally our jobs.

We're better together! The best work I have been involved with comes from great conversation and empathy. Both our jobs have unique quirks and nuances that make us defensive or feel insecure, but if we're working together we'll be better. Great creative can come from a Strategist and great strategy can come from a Creative. Our industry pushes us into boxes and ways of working that are the opposite of being creative, but jumping a a cliff together is less scary and can be more exhilarating!

Be less interested in being smart, and more interested in helping others get to great work. Nudge the creative if needed, don’t try to direct it. Be more accessible and available for chats with creatives.
Focus on the big picture
Don't say or write more than you need to. A strategy can be one word. It doesn't have to be 15 sildes.

I hate how people who don't understand strategy give their perspective like it's the divine revelation of God and they just cracked the Brand Challenge wide open, when in reality it just doesn't work because there is no research, no backing, no data, and no understanding behind what is said. Another thing is how after extensive sessions with Creatives they still make ideas that are completely opposite of the Strategy because they felt it was more powerful while the Strategic Perspective was significantly thought out with Research and had immense potential for Creative Work
Stop waiting for the creatives to come out with your greatest presentation lines.
keep the brief succinct
Fuck PowerPoint.
Strategists make me feel more confident about the ideas. Even easier and better when we get there together.
put yourself in the place of the human and not the consumer, understand his life and not his day of consumption, his real needs between family and not the marketing needs
When you all do your job well it makes ours so so much easier.
Just be insightful. Data is cool, but bring to the table those things that spark joy to you. It most likely will to ohers too.
We need each other to succeed!
I have your piece still framed on my wall. It is on every zoom I am on for years now.
Strategists and creatives should be in the same room working alongside eachother. Bring back old Ad-world style working, where a copywriter and designer we're teamed up.
I've said it all! Love it when they cheer the exciting, boundary pushing work, while keeping us on track.
the most effective briefs have. clear theme and direction when we open up too many avenues of exploration it's easy to get lost in witch path we're aiming to follow
You do your job, I’ll do mine.
sometimes you try to compete with us creatively
Be insightful and inspiring
Strategy is job function. It's like being a plumber. It's not a demarcation of your intelligence.  You're just a big dummy like the rest of us.
You're not the fucking boss. We're a team.
fuck off. really. stop trying to be a creative. stop trying to impose your thinking on the work. stop judging the work against the brief, start judging it against the world
Put yourself in a creative's shoe and be excited about ideas first then logic.
A great ad without any strategic derivation is still a great ad.
A great strategy can sell everything. That's cool. Yet some ideas do not to be overly explained by a long strategy. If you overly explain a joke, it's getting boring.
Take your time researching
Don‘t be too straightforward with knowledge and be more open to magic
Don't be too clever. If the brief is confusing, heady, wordy, it's an uphill battle from the beginning.
The world does not revolve around you
Advertising is grimy and gritty; too much strategy is "in the head." Don't be too smart for your own good.
i love you
Be a strategist, not a tactician.
go and live among, note down the behaviours
If you want creatives to better understand and interpret your strategies, teach them to better understand and interpret strategy.
Create better briefs

I’ve worked with bad strategists and I’ve worked with incredible ones. But at the end of the day, I don’t care how good you are at your job as long as you have a good attitude and you’re down to problem solve.
Haha, nothing. I have a lot of mutual trust and respect for strategists.
Dont wait until the end to brief design
